entries.end_notes: GIven the etymology and the evidence of most examples, this is a counter-charm against other magical spells. See D. Levene, "'This is a qyblˀ for Overturning Sorceries’: Form, Formula--Threads in a Web of Transmission", in G. Bohak, et al., eds, Continuity and Innovation in the Magical Tradition (Leiden: Brill, 2011) pp. 219ff. However in Jewish Aramaic Curse Texts from Late-Antique Mesopotamia (Brill, 2013) pp. 10ff. Levene goes further and suggests that since many of the qibla bowls are duplicate pairs that were sealed together by bitumen that the connection with the meaning "darkness" may be involved and that "counter" is not necessary.

entries.end_notes:
The transitive usage of the G forms is established by metrical considerations.
Given the semantics of the derived forms (compare English "provoke"), the basic meaning would appear to be "to summon together aloud", hence presumably related to ql "voice" and the Aramaic cognate to Arabic qāla "to say" and Akkadian qālu "to pay attention".

entries.primary_gloss: to rise; to stand; to last
entries.form_section: in JBA final mem often dropped, e.g. q)y and treated as if initial waw in the afel; see also s.v. q) adv. In JLA and related dialects the Ct (i.e. reflexive of the causative) )itAqaM is easily distinguished from the Dt )itqayayM ; not so in unvocalized Syriac where the form )tqym can be either stem in the earliest mss., although the Ct is often spelled with orthographic double taw or the taw's phonetic doubling explicitly marked with qushaya. In JLAtg the peal infinitive is irregular: m:qiym
entries.end_notes:
Many of the plethora of meanings ascribed to this verb in DJBA belong rather to the simple sense "to be present", while the special meaning is in fact conveyed by the preposition or the nominal relations.
The pael meaning "to swear", "make a covenant" may be understood as separate denominatives from qyām "covenant".
Students of Hebrew should note the difference between that language and Aramaic here: In BH קום is "to arise, stand up" while "to stand, stop" is עמד. In Aramaic קום is used for both of these senses.

entries.end_notes: Greek κύβος. The definition "hard stone, flint" given in SL from LS2 is yet another example of the plethora of falsely derived glosses in Brockelmann's praise-laden work. The main text of the Syro-hexapla is a translation of the Septuagint, it is not a translation of the Masoretic Text! Neither the Greek etymon of this loanword nor the meaning of the form it translates from the Septuagint supports such a gloss. Note, however, that even some of the most modern dictionaries of the Septuagint are not totally immune to the 19th century habit of adding a gloss to conform somewhat to the biblical text: see e.g. for the latter word GLS s.v. ψῆφος,-ου: "pebble, gravel Lam 3,16; grain (of sand) Sir 18,10; sharp stone (used as a knife) Ex 4,25; pebble used in voting, vote 4 Mc 15,26; account Eccl 7,25."

entries.form_section: Mand. gṭl; JLAtg. var. kTl! The Dt stem is not clearly attested in JLAtg, but the pl. perfect in JLAtg is always )itq:TalU instead of a form with final thematic /i/ vowel, which apparentlyâ€”as is the case with a few other strong verbsâ€”may represent a conflation of Gt with Dt. We have thus marked the Dt as common Aramaic, although there are no clear examples in that dialect.
entries.end_notes:
For the Palestinian usage "to behead" compare s.v. qdl.
Note the distinction between the G and D stems as taken up by BarBahlul extensively.
The two cited passages from GT AA Exod12 demonstrate an often overlooked grammatical pattern in PTA, i.e. that the derived stem infinitive when alone usually is of the Palestinian type with initial mem and final -ā but with pronominal suffixes follows the JLA pattern without mem and with final -ā replaced by -ūṯ_. This patterning was overlooked by DJPA by treating all these forms as nouns ending in -ū.

entries.primary_gloss: to turn into cloud, condense; C turn something into smoke
entries.form_section: CTLevi hqTyr is a Hebraizing replacement form for what must originally have appeared as )qTr in the Middle Aramaic original (see the GenAp exxs. This kind of change is common in this reworked material.
entries.end_notes: Syriac lexicographers, both native and modern, combined two etymologically separate roots 1) to tie into knots (=Akkadian kaṣāru, Heb. קשׁר) and 2) to burn incense (= Akk.qatāru, Heb. קטר ), that have here been disambiguated. All meanings involving the idea of "condensation" belong here, but the common semantic development in Syriac into "force" in the participial form qṭyr and related words is not easily assigned to one meaning or the other, hence, perhaps, the lexicographical confusion.

entries.end_notes: Possibly to be read as קלוטין, i.e., a pl. tant.. As shown by Amit Gvaryahu in Lesh 79 (2018):247ff., the Talmudic Hebrew equivalent of this expression refers to a special dispensation for excessive interest involved in overseas mercantile transactions, a usage probably influenced by (hardly a loanword!) the similar-sounding Latin term collocatum . One of these Semitic usages is obviously a play on the other, but which way the relationship goes is not clear.

entries.end_notes:
The emphasis in the DJBA entry for this word upon "happen to" is somewhat overdone; In many languages words for "to fall" develop into "to occur;" here the development is rather from "to be felled." Compare English "befall!" and cf. S. Kaufman, W. Leslau Fest..
The Syriac derived stem example in JerHex is clearly marked as C not D (pace LS/SL), which comports with its passive in Ct.
The sense "to drive cattle" is explained as a colloquial word by J. Furman in "The Zuqnin Chronicle as Evidence of Vernacular Aramaic in Eighth-Century Northern Mesopotamia", AS 18:2(2020):240-67.

entries.extra_comments: קרס ‘to become sour’ (DJBA 1045a). There is no firm evidence for MD’s QRS ‘to dry up; pa. to afflict, destroy’ (MD 416a). MD’s first citation has been extracted from its context, which is a series of incomprehensible magic words: btiksir qup ḏ-iaqup darqaqs harqup harqus ^a^qas qis qidsamia qaras blišanun giaiil iaubqa ṭuriaiil abrurna giaiil (DC 44:759–764). The second citation, also from DC 44 (1900–1901) reads ulmišdinun83 uʿl miqarsinun, but the parallel copies (de Morgan, DC 15) read ulmišdinun ulmiqarqilinun ulmiqarqisinun ‘to cast them down and overthrow them and encircle them’ (cf. MD 416, s.v. QRQL and QRQS). It would thus appear that DC 44’s reading results from haplography and that the existence of the verbal root q-r-s in Mandaic is unlikely.

entries.end_notes:
At Ezek 27:19 JLA uses the MT word קִדָּה, which is identified by some versions as "cassia;" but there is little reason to be certain of its meaning in our texts. The example from Geop is unhelpful since the meaning of the Greek is uncertain as well.
At JosephB 801:2 (not 810!) Sok/LS2 (p. 1314) renders "fetus" (following EN), giving the lemma as ܩܕܐ, but this makes little sense in the context. See, rather, Bedjan's extensive note there.

entries.end_notes: The repeated Sam. usage in Asatir 12 is based on Gen. 49:26: קדקד נזיר אחיו and should thus probably be treated as Hebrew.

At P Zeph 2:14 the original meaning (i.e., either "hedgehog" or bird) is somewhat unclear, although the parallelism suggests the latter. However, presumably based on the LXX ἐχῖνος, as well as the verbal root qpd , later tradition understood it as the former. In Kalilah wDimnah context suggests a bird, identified by 19th century scholars with NENA quptā "owl" (see Maclean, Dictionary 274) and PSm. At TgIs and P Is 34:15 context again suggests a bird.

const: Syr. qeryat, quwryat ; emph.: OfAEg., JLA, JPA qart.A) ; Syr. qry_tA) ; pl. qūrīn, qūrwīn qūryas: Qum.; qwry) , Syr., LJLA qw_r:yA) , OfAEast qry, PTA, LJLA qyrwyh , qwryyh , qwrwyyh , JLA qirwiyN NB: a mdynh is the large "capital" city of an administrative unit which can also be referred to by the same name/term, just as city-states in Antiquity were often called by the name of their capital city. In later periods a qryh is one of a number of large towns within such a unit, though in BA and earlier it can clearly be such a capital whereas mdynh refers to a province itself in such periods. By later times in Syriac it would seem that our word can also be used for any (larger?) unwalled settlement, i.e. "town" or "village", for which see U. Seidel, Altorientalische Forschungen 15(1988):143f., 152f. (but compare s.v. šṭḥ), or, as discussed below, for a "field".
See also s.v. qry #3 n.f.
The distinctive Syriac usage "field" is due to the similar semantic fields of the Greek underlying the early translations; compare the loanword ˀgwrs.
The various plurals in Syriac seem to be used differently by different authors; see, e.g., IshNum 114:29: ܐܓܘܪ̈ܣܐܼ ܩܘܪ̈ܝܣ܂ ܟܦܪ̈ܘܢܐܼ ܩܘܪ̈ܝܐ ܕܩܕܩ̈ܬܐ; i.e. the Greek-type plural is of "fields", the Aramaic-type "town".
